title: Don't Get Fooled
developmental_frame: At 11–12, children are increasingly online and surrounded by ads, headlines, and confident claims — but they tend to believe things that look official or that they *want* to be true. They're now ready for the crucial next step beyond Q1's 'investigate your own question': evaluating claims that other people are pushing at them, and asking 'who's telling me this, and why?'. This is real, immediately useful, and slightly thrilling — nobody likes being fooled.
authentic_value_meaning: Value here is not being anyone's fool — seeing through a misleading ad, a clickbait headline, or a too-good-to-be-true claim, and knowing how to check before believing. It's the beginning of thinking for yourself instead of swallowing whatever you're told.

motivation_lever.mechanism: The child learns to think for themselves rather than be told what to believe — and at this age, being 'nobody's fool', the one who spots the trick, feels genuinely powerful and a little cool. Catching a real ad or headline trying to manipulate them turns critical thinking into a satisfying game of not-getting-caught.

artifact: A 'fool-proofing' breakdown of three real claims, ads, or headlines the child found, each showing the trick being used and how they checked whether it was true.
success_criteria: For each claim, the child identified who was saying it and what they wanted.
They named the specific trick or missing information in each.
They checked at least one claim against another source before deciding.

lessons.title: Who's telling you, and why?
lessons.concept: Before believing a claim, ask two questions: who is saying it, and what do they want? An ad wants your money. A headline wants your click. Knowing the motive behind a message tells you how carefully to check it.
lessons.real_example: 'Dentists recommend this toothpaste!' — but the ad was paid for by the toothpaste company. Who's telling you (the seller) and why (to sell) changes how much you should trust it.
lessons.apply_task: Find three real claims (ads, headlines, posts). For each, write who's saying it and what you think they want.
lessons.reflection_prompt: Did knowing the 'why' change how much you believed any of them? Motive is a big clue.
lessons.feedback_task: Show your three to a parent and compare your guesses about who benefits.
lessons.teach_back_task: Show a friend how to ask 'who's telling me and why?' about an ad they've seen.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: AI can also have a 'why' — it's trained to sound confident even when unsure. Ask 'who/why' about AI answers too, and never treat a confident tone as proof.
success_criteria: Three real claims are analysed for source and motive.

lessons.title: Spot the common tricks
lessons.concept: Misleading messages use a few tricks over and over: exaggeration ('the BEST ever!'), fake urgency ('only 2 left!'), missing information (leaving out the bad bits), and cherry-picking (showing only the numbers that help them). Once you know the tricks, you see them everywhere.
lessons.real_example: '9 out of 10 people loved it!' sounds great — until you ask how many people they asked. If it was 10 friends of the seller, the number means nothing. That's cherry-picking plus missing information.
lessons.apply_task: Look at your three claims again and name the trick each one uses (or admit if one seems honest).
lessons.reflection_prompt: Which trick fools you most easily? Knowing your own weak spot helps you guard it.
lessons.feedback_task: Ask a parent if they can spot a trick you missed in your three claims.
lessons.teach_back_task: Teach someone the four common tricks with an example of each.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: AI can help you name the tricks in an ad — a fair use. But spotting them yourself first builds the instinct that protects you when no AI is around.
success_criteria: Each claim's trick (or honesty) is identified.

lessons.title: Check before you believe
lessons.concept: The real defence is checking. Cross-check a claim against a different, independent source — one that doesn't benefit from it. If you can't find the claim anywhere trustworthy, that's a red flag.
lessons.real_example: A post says a certain food is 'poison'. You check a couple of reliable health sources — none agree. The claim doesn't survive the check, so you don't spread it.
lessons.apply_task: Pick your most doubtful claim and check it against at least one independent source. Write what you found.
lessons.reflection_prompt: Was the claim true, false, or somewhere in between? How hard was it to find a trustworthy check?
lessons.feedback_task: Show your checking to an adult and ask if your source was a good, independent one.
lessons.teach_back_task: Show someone how to cross-check a surprising claim before believing it.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: AI can point you toward sources, but it sometimes makes things up ('hallucinates'). Always confirm with a real, findable source — don't let a confident AI answer be your only check.
success_criteria: At least one claim was cross-checked against an independent source.

lessons.title: Share what you found
lessons.concept: Put it together: present your three claims, the tricks they used, and what your checking revealed. Teaching others to spot tricks is how you protect your friends and family too — being fool-proof is more useful when it spreads.
lessons.real_example: You show your family: 'This ad exaggerates, this headline uses fake urgency, and this so-called fact turned out to be false when I checked.' Now they're a bit harder to fool too.
lessons.apply_task: Finish your fool-proofing breakdown of all three claims and present it to your family.
lessons.reflection_prompt: Which of your three was the sneakiest? What will you check more carefully from now on?
lessons.feedback_task: Ask your audience which breakdown surprised them most. Note it.
lessons.teach_back_task: Walk a family member through all three claims and how you saw through them.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: The judgement of what's misleading is yours, built from your own checking — that's the skill worth having. Share it in your own words.
success_criteria: A three-claim breakdown was completed and presented.
The child can explain the trick and the check for each.
